How to use Claude/

what type of ai projects can I build to showcase my ai skills in github

20:22

Great question, Jas! Given your development setup and interest in AI, here are some project ideas that would make a strong GitHub portfolio:

Practical AI tools — build things people can actually use, like a CLI tool that summarizes PDFs, a code review assistant, or a document Q&A system. These show you can ship real products, not just notebooks.

RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) applications — build a system that indexes a knowledge base (docs, wikis, codebases) and answers questions with citations. This is one of the most in-demand AI skills right now and demonstrates you understand embeddings, vector databases, and prompt engineering.

AI agents — create an agent that can break down tasks and use tools (web search, file operations, API calls) to accomplish goals. For example, a research agent that takes a topic and produces a structured report, or a coding agent that fixes bugs in a repo.

Fine-tuning or evaluation projects — fine-tune an open-source model on a specific domain, or build an evaluation framework that benchmarks LLM performance on a task. Evals especially are underrated portfolio pieces.

Full-stack AI apps — a web app with an AI backend, like a meeting notes summarizer, a recipe generator from fridge photos, or a personal finance advisor. These show you can integrate AI into real products with proper UX.

Data pipelines with AI — ETL pipelines that use LLMs for classification, extraction, or transformation. For instance, scraping job listings and using AI to categorize and score them.

A few tips for making these stand out on GitHub: write a solid README with screenshots or a demo GIF, include clear setup instructions, add tests, and explain your architectural decisions. Recruiters and hiring managers often skim READMEs more than code.

Would you like me to help you scope out any of these in more detail, or sketch out a project structure for one that interests you?

any around cyber security

20:23

Great direction — cybersecurity + AI is a hot niche with real demand and relatively few portfolio projects out there. Here are some ideas:

Phishing email detector — build a classifier that analyzes emails and flags phishing attempts. You could use an LLM to explain why an email looks suspicious (spoofed domains, urgency tactics, mismatched links). Train or fine-tune on public phishing datasets like the Nazario phishing corpus.

Log anomaly detector — ingest system or network logs and use ML to spot anomalies. You could combine traditional approaches (isolation forests, autoencoders) with an LLM layer that generates human-readable explanations of what the anomaly might mean. Great for showing you understand both classical ML and LLMs.

Vulnerability report summarizer — a tool that takes CVE feeds or security advisories and produces prioritized, plain-English summaries tailored to a specific tech stack. This is a practical RAG project with a security twist.

Malicious URL/domain classifier — train a model to classify URLs as benign or malicious based on features like domain age, URL structure, TLS cert info, and WHOIS data. Add an API layer so it works as a real-time lookup service.

Threat intelligence dashboard — aggregate OSINT feeds (CVEs, threat actor reports, exploit databases) and use AI to cluster, summarize, and surface emerging threats. A full-stack project that shows data engineering + AI + visualization.

Security policy Q&A bot — a RAG system that ingests compliance frameworks (NIST, ISO 27001, SOC 2) and lets users ask natural language questions like "what do I need for access control compliance?" Useful and very demo-friendly.

AI-powered SIEM triage assistant — simulate a security operations workflow where an AI agent triages alerts, correlates events, and recommends response actions. You could use synthetic alert data and build it as an interactive dashboard.

Prompt injection detector — very meta and topical. Build a classifier that detects prompt injection attempts in user inputs to LLM applications. This shows awareness of AI-specific security concerns, which is a differentiator.

The prompt injection detector and phishing detector are probably the easiest to start with and the most eye-catching on a resume. The threat intelligence dashboard or SIEM assistant would be more ambitious but really impressive.

Want me to help you plan one of these out — architecture, tech stack, repo structure?

yes I think this is a good candidate Threat intelligence dashboard — aggregate OSINT feeds (CVEs, threat actor reports, exploit databases) and use AI to cluster, summarize, and surface emerging threats. A full-stack project that shows data engineering + AI + visualization.

20:27

Awesome choice, Jas — this one hits a lot of skills at once. Let me put together a solid project plan for you.

Threat Intelligence Dashboard — Project Plan

What it does

Aggregates open-source threat intelligence feeds, uses AI to analyze/cluster/summarize threats, and presents everything in a real-time dashboard. Think of it as a personal SOC analyst that watches the threat landscape for you.

Tech Stack

Backend: Python (FastAPI) — lightweight, async, great for API work Frontend: React with a charting library (Recharts or D3) Database: PostgreSQL for structured data + a vector DB (ChromaDB or Pinecone) for semantic search AI layer: Claude API or OpenAI for summarization/clustering, plus sentence-transformers for embeddings Data pipeline: Python scripts with scheduling (APScheduler or cron) Deployment: Docker Compose so anyone can spin it up

OSINT Data Sources (all free)

  • NVD/CVE feed — NIST's vulnerability database API
  • CISA KEV — Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog
  • AlienVault OTX — threat indicators and pulses
  • Abuse.ch — malware/botnet threat feeds (URLhaus, ThreatFox)
  • MITRE ATT&CK — tactics/techniques mapping

Core Features (build in this order)

Phase 1 — Data ingestion pipeline

  • Fetch and normalize data from 2-3 feeds into a common schema
  • Store in PostgreSQL with proper indexing
  • Scheduled refresh (hourly/daily)

Phase 2 — AI analysis layer

  • Generate embeddings for each threat entry
  • Cluster related threats using semantic similarity
  • Use an LLM to generate plain-English summaries per cluster
  • Auto-tag threats with MITRE ATT&CK categories

Phase 3 — API and search

  • REST API endpoints: list threats, search, filter by severity/type/date
  • Semantic search: "show me recent ransomware activity targeting healthcare"
  • Trending threats endpoint (what's spiking this week)

Phase 4 — Dashboard UI

  • Threat feed timeline with severity color-coding
  • Cluster view showing related threats grouped together
  • Trend charts (threat volume over time, by category)
  • Search bar with natural language queries
  • Detail panel with AI-generated summaries

Repo Structure

threat-intel-dashboard/
├── README.md
├── docker-compose.yml
├── backend/
│   ├── app/
│   │   ├── main.py            # FastAPI app
│   │   ├── models/            # DB models
│   │   ├── routers/           # API endpoints
│   │   ├── services/
│   │   │   ├── ingestion/     # Feed fetchers
│   │   │   ├── ai/            # Clustering, summarization
│   │   │   └── search/        # Semantic + keyword search
│   │   └── config.py
│   ├── requirements.txt
│   └── Dockerfile
├── frontend/
│   ├── src/
│   │   ├── components/        # Dashboard widgets
│   │   ├── pages/
│   │   └── hooks/
│   ├── package.json
│   └── Dockerfile
└── scripts/
    └── seed_data.py           # Initial data load

What makes this impressive on GitHub

  • Real data, not toy examples — live feeds make it feel production-grade
  • Multiple AI techniques — embeddings, clustering, summarization, classification
  • Full stack — data engineering → AI → API → UI
  • Docker Compose — anyone can clone and run it in minutes
  • Good README — architecture diagram, screenshots, demo GIF

README must-haves

  • Architecture diagram showing the data flow
  • Screenshots of the dashboard
  • Clear setup instructions (ideally just docker-compose up)
  • List of data sources with links
  • Explanation of the AI pipeline
  • Future improvements section (shows you think ahead)

Want me to scaffold out any specific phase — like the data ingestion scripts or the FastAPI skeleton? I can also help you write the README template.

I'm not a coder by any means so can you guide me throughout the process of building this?

20:29

Of course! We'll build this step by step together. Since you're not coming from a coding background, we'll start small and layer on complexity as you get comfortable.

Before we dive in, let me understand where you're at so I can tailor the guidance properly.

Once I know these, I'll map out a learning path and we'll start with Phase 0 — getting your environment set up and building the simplest possible version first (just fetching one CVE feed and printing results to the terminal). Small wins to build momentum!

Q: How comfortable are you with Python? A: Tried basics (variables, loops)

Q: Do you have these installed on your Mac? (Select all that apply) A: Python 3, Node.js / npm

Q: How do you prefer to learn? A: Explain concepts then show code

20:31

Perfect — you've got a solid starting point. You have the core tools installed, you know Python basics, and you like understanding the why before the how. That's actually the best way to learn this.

Here's how we'll approach the build:

Our Learning Path

Phase 0 — Project setup & your first API call (today) Phase 1 — Data ingestion pipeline (fetch & store threat data) Phase 2 — AI analysis layer (embeddings, clustering, summaries) Phase 3 — Backend API (serve data to the frontend) Phase 4 — Dashboard UI (visualize everything)

Each phase will follow the same pattern: I explain the concept, show you the code, you run it, and we iterate. We won't move on until you're comfortable.

Phase 0 — Project Setup & Your First API Call

Let's start with two goals: set up the project folder structure, and successfully fetch real CVE data from the internet. By the end of this phase you'll have a Python script that pulls live vulnerability data and prints it in a readable format.

Step 1: Create the project folder

Open your terminal and type these commands one at a time:

bash
cd ~/orogen.ai
mkdir threat-intel-dashboard
cd threat-intel-dashboard
mkdir -p backend/app/services/ingestion
touch backend/app/__init__.py
touch backend/app/services/__init__.py
touch backend/app/services/ingestion/__init__.py

What's happening: We're creating the project folder and some subfolders. The __init__.py files are empty files that tell Python "this folder is a package you can import from." Think of them as labels that say "there's code in here."

Step 2: Create a virtual environment

bash
python3 -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate

What's happening: A virtual environment is like a sandbox for your project. Any Python packages you install will only live inside this project, so they don't mess with anything else on your Mac. You'll know it's active when you see (venv) at the start of your terminal line.

Step 3: Install our first dependency

bash
pip install requests

What's happening: requests is a Python library that makes it easy to fetch data from the internet (APIs, websites, etc.). It's the most popular Python package for this — almost every Python developer uses it.

Step 4: Write your first feed fetcher

Now create the file that will pull real CVE data. Open your code editor (if you have VS Code, type code . in the terminal) and create a new file at:

backend/app/services/ingestion/nvd_fetcher.py

Put this code in it:

python
import requests
from datetime import datetime, timedelta


def fetch_recent_cves(days_back=7):
    """
    Fetch recent CVEs from the National Vulnerability Database.
    
    The NVD is a free, public API run by NIST (the National Institute 
    of Standards and Technology). It contains every publicly disclosed 
    vulnerability, each with a unique CVE ID like 'CVE-2024-12345'.
    """
    
    # Calculate the date range — we want the last 7 days of CVEs
    end_date = datetime.now()
    start_date = end_date - timedelta(days=days_back)
    
    # Format dates the way the NVD API expects them
    date_format = "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S.000"
    
    # Build the API URL with our parameters
    base_url = "https://services.nvd.nist.gov/rest/json/cves/2.0"
    params = {
        "pubStartDate": start_date.strftime(date_format),
        "pubEndDate": end_date.strftime(date_format),
        "resultsPerPage": 10  # Start small — just 10 results
    }
    
    print(f"Fetching CVEs from the last {days_back} days...")
    print(f"URL: {base_url}")
    
    # Make the API request — this is where we actually call the NVD
    response = requests.get(base_url, params=params)
    
    # Check if the request was successful (status code 200 = OK)
    if response.status_code != 200:
        print(f"Error: Got status code {response.status_code}")
        return []
    
    # Parse the JSON response into a Python dictionary
    data = response.json()
    
    # Extract just the parts we care about from each CVE
    cves = []
    for item in data.get("vulnerabilities", []):
        cve_data = item.get("cve", {})
        
        # Get the description (it's nested in a list)
        descriptions = cve_data.get("descriptions", [])
        description = "No description available"
        for desc in descriptions:
            if desc.get("lang") == "en":
                description = desc.get("value", description)
                break
        
        # Get the severity score if available
        metrics = cve_data.get("metrics", {})
        severity = "Unknown"
        score = None
        
        # Try CVSS v3.1 first, then v3.0
        for version in ["cvssMetricV31", "cvssMetricV30"]:
            if version in metrics:
                cvss = metrics[version][0].get("cvssData", {})
                severity = cvss.get("baseSeverity", "Unknown")
                score = cvss.get("baseScore", None)
                break
        
        cves.append({
            "id": cve_data.get("id"),
            "description": description,
            "severity": severity,
            "score": score,
            "published": cve_data.get("published"),
        })
    
    return cves


# This block only runs when you execute this file directly
if __name__ == "__main__":
    cves = fetch_recent_cves()
    
    print(f"\nFound {len(cves)} CVEs:\n")
    print("-" * 80)
    
    for cve in cves:
        print(f"ID:       {cve['id']}")
        print(f"Severity: {cve['severity']} (Score: {cve['score']})")
        print(f"Published: {cve['published']}")
        print(f"Description: {cve['description'][:200]}...")
        print("-" * 80)

Step 5: Run it!

Back in your terminal (make sure you're in the threat-intel-dashboard folder and your venv is active):

bash
python backend/app/services/ingestion/nvd_fetcher.py

You should see real CVE data printed in your terminal — actual vulnerabilities from the last week. That's live threat intelligence you just pulled.

What you just learned:

  • How to call a REST API with Python
  • How to parse JSON responses and extract nested data
  • How the NVD structures vulnerability data
  • The basics of CVE IDs and CVSS severity scores

Go ahead and run it, and let me know what you see! If you hit any errors, paste them here and we'll work through them together. Once this is working, we'll add a second feed (CISA KEV) and then start storing the data in a database.

with step 4 how can I get this to work in my vs code? should I create a github repo and clone ?

20:38

Good thinking — yes, let's set up a GitHub repo properly from the start. That way your work is version-controlled and visible on your profile from day one.

Here's the workflow:

Step 1: Create the repo on GitHub

  1. Go to github.com and log in
  2. Click the + icon (top right) → New repository
  3. Fill in:
    • Repository name: threat-intel-dashboard
    • Description: AI-powered threat intelligence dashboard that aggregates OSINT feeds, clusters threats, and surfaces emerging risks
    • Public (so recruiters can see it)
    • Check Add a README file
    • Add .gitignore → select Python from the dropdown
  4. Click Create repository

Step 2: Clone it to your Mac

Back in your terminal:

bash
cd ~/orogen.ai
git clone https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/threat-intel-dashboard.git
cd threat-intel-dashboard

Replace YOUR-USERNAME with your actual GitHub username. This downloads the repo and puts you inside it.

Step 3: Open it in VS Code

bash
code .
```

This opens the whole project folder in VS Code. You'll see the README and .gitignore already there.

### Step 4: Create the folders and files in VS Code

Now instead of using the terminal to create files, you can use VS Code's sidebar. Click the file explorer icon (top-left) and create this structure by right-clicking → **New Folder** / **New File**:
```
threat-intel-dashboard/
├── backend/
│   └── app/
│       ├── __init__.py
│       └── services/
│           ├── __init__.py
│           └── ingestion/
│               ├── __init__.py
│               └── nvd_fetcher.py

The __init__.py files can be empty — just create them and leave them blank. Then paste the code from my previous message into nvd_fetcher.py.

Step 5: Set up the virtual environment and install requests

In VS Code, open the integrated terminal with Ctrl + ` (backtick) or go to Terminal → New Terminal from the menu. Then run:

bash
python3 -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate
pip install requests

Step 6: Run the script

Still in that same terminal:

bash
python backend/app/services/ingestion/nvd_fetcher.py

Step 7: Push your work to GitHub

Once the script runs successfully, save your progress:

bash
git add .
git commit -m "Phase 0: Add NVD CVE fetcher - first data source"
git push

This uploads your code to GitHub. Get in the habit of committing after each working milestone — it builds a nice commit history that shows your progress.

One quick tip: when VS Code asks if you want to install the Python extension, say yes. It gives you syntax highlighting, error detection, and lets you run scripts with a play button instead of typing commands.

Give this a go and let me know when you see CVE data in your terminal, or if you hit any snags along the way!

I've cloned it on my mac but how can I open it in vscode

20:45

You've got a couple of options:

Option 1: From the terminal

Navigate to the project folder and open it:

bash
cd ~/orogen.ai/threat-intel-dashboard
code .

If code doesn't work, you need to install the VS Code command line tool first. Open VS Code manually, then press Cmd + Shift + P, type "Shell Command: Install 'code' command in PATH", and hit Enter. Then try code . again.

Option 2: From VS Code directly

  1. Open VS Code
  2. Go to File → Open Folder
  3. Navigate to /Users/jasimalnajjar/orogen.ai/threat-intel-dashboard
  4. Click Open

Either way gets you to the same place — your project folder open in VS Code with the file explorer on the left. Option 1 is faster once it's set up, but Option 2 always works.

Let me know once you've got it open and we'll move on to creating the folder structure and files!

can't run the fetcher py (venv) jasimalnajjar@MacBookPro services % python backend/app/services/ingestion/nvd_fetcher.py /Users/jasimalnajjar/threat-intel-dashboard/venv/bin/python: can't open file '/Users/jasimalnajjar/threat-intel-dashboard/backend/app/services/backend/app/services/ingestion/nvd_fetcher.py': [Errno 2] No such file or directory

20:53

The issue is that your terminal is already inside the services folder, so when you type the full path it's looking in the wrong place. You need to run the command from the project root.

Run this:

bash
cd ~/orogen.ai/threat-intel-dashboard
python backend/app/services/ingestion/nvd_fetcher.py

The first line takes you back to the top of your project, and the second runs the script from there. You can always check where you are by typing pwd (print working directory).

Let me know what you see!

pwd

In VS Code:

  1. In the file explorer sidebar on the left, navigate to backend → app → services → ingestion
  2. Right-click on the ingestion folder

  5. Paste in the code from my earlier message (the full script starting with import requests)

Then go back to your terminal, make sure you're in the project root:

bash
cd ~/orogen.ai/threat-intel-dashboard
python backend/app/services/ingestion/nvd_fetcher.py

Let me know what happens!

(venv) jasimalnajjar@MacBookPro ingestion % python backend/app/services/ingestion/nvd_fetcher.py /Users/jasimalnajjar/threat-intel-dashboard/venv/bin/python: can't open file '/Users/jasimalnajjar/threat-intel-dashboard/backend/app/services/ingestion/backend/app/services/ingestion/nvd_fetcher.py': [Errno 2] No such file or directory

20:58

You're still inside the ingestion folder — see how the path is doubling up? You need to go back to the project root first:

bash
cd ~/orogen.ai/threat-intel-dashboard

Then run:

python backend/app/services/ingestion/nvd_fetcher.py

The key is always running those two commands separately — first navigate, then run.

ok that worked now what next?

20:59

Awesome — you just pulled live threat intelligence data! That's a big first milestone.

Let's save your progress to GitHub, then add a second data source.

Step 1: Push to GitHub

bash
git add .
git commit -m "Phase 0: Add NVD CVE fetcher - first data source"
git push

Step 2: Add the CISA KEV feed

The CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog is a list of vulnerabilities that are actively being exploited in the wild. It's a much smaller, higher-priority list compared to the full NVD — think of it as the "these are the ones you need to worry about right now" list.

In VS Code, right-click the ingestion folder → New File → name it cisa_fetcher.py and paste this:

python
import requests


def fetch_kev():
    """
    Fetch CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog.
    
    Unlike the NVD which lists ALL vulnerabilities, this catalog 
    only lists ones that are confirmed to be actively exploited 
    by attackers. If a CVE is on this list, it's urgent.
    """
    
    url = "https://www.cisa.gov/sites/default/files/feeds/known_exploited_vulnerabilities.json"
    
    print("Fetching CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities...")
    
    response = requests.get(url)
    
    if response.status_code != 200:
        print(f"Error: Got status code {response.status_code}")
        return []
    
    data = response.json()
    
    # Get the 10 most recently added vulnerabilities
    vulnerabilities = data.get("vulnerabilities", [])
    recent = vulnerabilities[-10:]  # Last 10 entries are the newest
    
    kevs = []
    for vuln in recent:
        kevs.append({
            "id": vuln.get("cveID"),
            "name": vuln.get("vulnerabilityName"),
            "vendor": vuln.get("vendorProject"),
            "product": vuln.get("product"),
            "description": vuln.get("shortDescription"),
            "date_added": vuln.get("dateAdded"),
            "due_date": vuln.get("dueDate"),
            "source": "CISA KEV"
        })
    
    return kevs


if __name__ == "__main__":
    kevs = fetch_kev()
    
    print(f"\nFound {len(kevs)} recently added KEVs:\n")
    print("-" * 80)
    
    for kev in kevs:
        print(f"ID:       {kev['id']}")
        print(f"Name:     {kev['name']}")
        print(f"Vendor:   {kev['vendor']} - {kev['product']}")
        print(f"Added:    {kev['date_added']}")
        print(f"Fix by:   {kev['due_date']}")
        print(f"Description: {kev['description'][:200]}")
        print("-" * 80)

Step 3: Run it

bash
cd ~/orogen.ai/threat-intel-dashboard
python backend/app/services/ingestion/cisa_fetcher.py

Step 4: Push again

bash
git add .
git commit -m "Phase 0: Add CISA KEV fetcher - second data source"
git push

Once this works, you'll have two live threat feeds. The next step will be building a database to store all this data so it persists between runs. Let me know how it goes!

(venv) jasimalnajjar@MacBookPro threat-intel-dashboard % git add fatal: not a git repository (or any of the parent directories): .git

21:00

Looks like you cloned the repo to a different location than where you're working now. Let's check:

bash
pwd

And then:

bash
ls ~/orogen.ai/threat-intel-dashboard/.git

Paste what both of those show. My guess is you have two threat-intel-dashboard folders — one inside ~/orogen.ai/ (where you cloned) and one at ~/ (where you've been working). We'll sort it out and get everything in one place.

(venv) jasimalnajjar@MacBookPro threat-intel-dashboard % ls backend threat-intel-dashboard venv

21:01

There it is — the git repo is nested inside. You cloned threat-intel-dashboard into another threat-intel-dashboard folder. So your actual repo with .git is at:

~/orogen.ai/threat-intel-dashboard/threat-intel-dashboard/

Let's fix this. We'll move everything into the correct repo folder:

bash
cp -r backend/* threat-intel-dashboard/backend/
cp -r venv threat-intel-dashboard/
cd threat-intel-dashboard

Now confirm you're in the git repo:

bash
git status

You should see a list of files showing as new/modified. If that works, go ahead and push:

bash
git add .
git commit -m "Phase 0: Add NVD and CISA fetchers"
git push

Then from now on, always work from this folder. You can open it in VS Code with:

bash
code .

Let me know what git status shows!

21:03

That means the files didn't copy over. Let's check what's actually in each folder:

bash
ls backend/app/services/ingestion/

Then:

bash
cd ..

21:17

I totally understand the frustration — the folder mess has made this way harder than it needs to be. Unfortunately I can't control your VS Code directly.

But here's what I can do: let's start clean and get everything right in one go. It'll take 5 minutes and save us headaches later.

Step 1: Delete the messy folder

bash
cd ~
rm -rf ~/threat-intel-dashboard

Step 2: Clone the repo fresh

bash
git clone https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/threat-intel-dashboard.git
cd threat-intel-dashboard

Replace YOUR-USERNAME with your GitHub username.

Step 3: Open in VS Code

bash
code .

Step 4: Set up the environment

In VS Code's terminal (Terminal → New Terminal):

bash
python3 -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate
pip install requests

Step 5: Create all folders and files

Still in VS Code's terminal:

bash
mkdir -p backend/app/services/ingestion
touch backend/app/__init__.py
touch backend/app/services/__init__.py
touch backend/app/services/ingestion/__init__.py

Step 6: Create the files in VS Code

In the sidebar, you should now see the folder structure. Right-click ingestion → New File → nvd_fetcher.py → paste the code. Do the same for cisa_fetcher.py.

Step 7: Test and push

bash
python backend/app/services/ingestion/nvd_fetcher.py
python backend/app/services/ingestion/cisa_fetcher.py
git add .
git commit -m "Phase 0: Add NVD and CISA threat feed fetchers"
git push

Take it one step at a time and let me know after each one if anything looks off. We'll get this sorted!

21:27

The CISA API might be slow or the URL might have changed. Let's check what's happening. Open cisa_fetcher.py and add a quick debug line. Replace the if response.status_code != 200: section so it looks like this:

python
    response = requests.get(url)
    
    print(f"Status code: {response.status_code}")
    
    if response.status_code != 200:
        print(f"Error: Got status code {response.status_code}")
        return []

Save it and run again:

python backend/app/services/ingestion/cisa_fetcher.py

What does it print? If it shows nothing at all (not even "Fetching CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities..."), then the file might be empty. Check it with:

bash
cat backend/app/services/ingestion/cisa_fetcher.py

Paste what you see.

ok I've officially cloned the repos and good to move on. Both cisa and nvd fetche is available

21:31

Let's push what you have and move to Phase 1.

bash
git add .
git commit -m "Phase 0: Add NVD and CISA threat feed fetchers"
git push

Phase 1 — Storing Data in a Database

Right now, your fetchers pull data and print it to the terminal, then it's gone. We need to store it so the dashboard can access it later. We'll use SQLite for now — it's a database that lives in a single file on your machine, no setup required, and Python has it built in.

The concept

A database is like a spreadsheet. We'll create a table called threats where each row is one vulnerability. The columns will be things like cve_id, description, severity, source, etc.

Step 1: Install SQLAlchemy

SQLAlchemy is a Python library that lets you work with databases using Python code instead of writing raw SQL. Think of it as a translator between Python and the database.

bash
pip install sqlalchemy

Step 2: Create the database model

This defines the structure of our threats table. Create a new file at backend/app/models.py:

python
from sqlalchemy import create_engine, Column, String, Float, DateTime, Text
from sqlalchemy.orm import declarative_base, sessionmaker
from datetime import datetime

# This creates the base class that all our database tables will inherit from
Base = declarative_base()


class Threat(Base):
    """
    Each row in this table represents one threat/vulnerability.
    Think of it like a row in a spreadsheet.
    """
    __tablename__ = "threats"

    # Each column in our "spreadsheet"
    id = Column(String, primary_key=True)          # CVE ID like "CVE-2024-12345"
    source = Column(String)                         # "NVD" or "CISA KEV"
    description = Column(Text)                      # What the vulnerability is
    severity = Column(String)                       # "CRITICAL", "HIGH", etc.
    score = Column(Float, nullable=True)            # CVSS score like 9.8
    published = Column(String)                      # When it was published
    vendor = Column(String, nullable=True)          # Affected vendor
    product = Column(String, nullable=True)         # Affected product
    date_added = Column(String, nullable=True)      # When added to feed
    fetched_at = Column(DateTime, default=datetime.now)  # When we fetched it


# Set up the database connection — this creates a file called threats.db
engine = create_engine("sqlite:///threats.db")

# Create the table if it doesn't exist yet
Base.metadata.create_all(engine)

# Session is how we read/write data — think of it as opening the spreadsheet
Session = sessionmaker(bind=engine)


def get_session():
    return Session()

Step 3: Create a script that fetches AND stores

Create a new file at backend/app/services/ingestion/run_ingestion.py:

python
from backend.app.models import Threat, get_session
from backend.app.services.ingestion.nvd_fetcher import fetch_recent_cves
from backend.app.services.ingestion.cisa_fetcher import fetch_kev


def store_threats(threats, source):
    """
    Takes a list of threat dictionaries and saves them to the database.
    If a threat with the same ID already exists, it skips it (no duplicates).
    """
    session = get_session()
    added = 0

    for threat in threats:
        # Check if this CVE is already in the database
        exists = session.query(Threat).filter_by(id=threat["id"]).first()
        if exists:
            continue

        new_threat = Threat(
            id=threat["id"],
            source=source,
            description=threat.get("description", ""),
            severity=threat.get("severity", "Unknown"),
            score=threat.get("score"),
            published=threat.get("published", ""),
            vendor=threat.get("vendor"),
            product=threat.get("product"),
            date_added=threat.get("date_added"),
        )
        session.add(new_threat)
        added += 1

    session.commit()
    session.close()
    print(f"  Added {added} new threats from {source}")


def run_ingestion():
    """Fetch from all sources and store everything."""

    print("Starting ingestion...\n")

    # Fetch and store NVD data
    print("1. Fetching NVD CVEs...")
    cves = fetch_recent_cves()
    store_threats(cves, source="NVD")

    # Fetch and store CISA KEV data
    print("\n2. Fetching CISA KEVs...")
    kevs = fetch_kev()
    store_threats(kevs, source="CISA KEV")

    # Show what's in the database now
    session = get_session()
    total = session.query(Threat).count()
    session.close()
    print(f"\nDone! Total threats in database: {total}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    run_ingestion()

Step 4: Run the ingestion

From the project root:

bash
python -m backend.app.services.ingestion.run_ingestion

You should see it fetch from both sources and tell you how many threats were stored. Run it a second time — it should say "Added 0 new threats" because it won't duplicate entries.

Step 5: Push to GitHub

bash
git add .
git commit -m "Phase 1: Add database storage with SQLAlchemy"
git push

What you just learned:

  • What a database model is (defining table structure in Python)
  • How SQLAlchemy translates Python objects into database rows
  • How to prevent duplicate entries
  • The concept of a data pipeline: fetch → transform → store

Give it a try and paste what you see when you run it!

(venv) jasimalnajjar@MacBookPro threat-intel-dashboard % git push To https://github.com/jasimalnajjar/threat-intel-dashboard.git ! [rejected] main -> main (fetch first) error: failed to push some refs to 'https://github.com/jasimalnajjar/threat-intel-dashboard.git' hint: Updates were rejected because the remote contains work that you do hint: not have locally. This is usually caused by another repository pushing hint: to the same ref. You may want to first integrate the remote changes hint: (e.g., 'git pull ...') before pushing again. hint: See the 'Note about fast-forwards' in 'git push --help' for details. (venv) jasimalnajjar@MacBookPro threat-intel-dashboard %

21:32

This means GitHub has changes (probably from the old repo setup) that your local copy doesn't. Easy fix:

bash
git pull --rebase
git push

If it opens a text editor with a merge message, just type :wq and press Enter to save and close it.

Let me know if it goes through!

Step 2: Create the database model This defines the structure of our threats table. Create a new file at backend/app/models.py:

Let me know once both files are created and saved!

21:37

Now let's run the ingestion to make sure the database works:

bash
python -m backend.app.services.ingestion.run_ingestion

This should fetch from both NVD and CISA and store everything in a database file called threats.db. Paste what you see!

(venv) jasimalnajjar@MacBookPro threat-intel-dashboard % python -m backend.app.services.ingestion.run_ingestion Starting ingestion... 1. Fetching NVD CVEs... Fetching CVEs from the last 7 days... URL: https://services.nvd.nist.gov/rest/json/cves/2.0 Added 0 new threats from NVD 2. Fetching CISA KEVs... Fetching CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities... Added 0 new threats from CISA KEV Done! Total threats in database: 20

21:38

It's working! The database already has 20 threats stored from your earlier runs, and it correctly skipped duplicates ("Added 0 new threats"). That's exactly the behavior we want.

Let's push and move on to Phase 2 — this is where it gets exciting because we add the AI layer.

bash
git add .
git commit -m "Phase 1: Database storing threats successfully"
git push

Phase 2 — The AI Analysis Layer

Right now we have raw data sitting in a database. Phase 2 makes it intelligent. We'll use an LLM (Claude) to:

  1. Summarize threats in plain English
  2. Categorize them by attack type (ransomware, data breach, etc.)
  3. Assess risk and prioritize what matters most

The concept

Instead of reading through raw CVE descriptions, we'll send them to Claude's API and get back structured analysis. Imagine a security analyst reading every vulnerability and writing a brief — that's what we're automating.

Step 1: Get a Claude API key

  1. Go to console.anthropic.com
  2. Sign up or log in
  3. Go to API Keys
  4. Click Create Key
  5. Copy the key — it'll look like sk-ant-...

Step 2: Install dependencies

bash
pip install anthropic python-dotenv
```

### Step 3: Create a .env file

In VS Code, right-click on the **root** of your project (the top-level `threat-intel-dashboard` folder) → **New File** → name it `.env` → add this:
```
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-your-key-here
```

Replace with your actual key. This file keeps your key secret — never commit it to GitHub.

Now make sure it's in your `.gitignore`. Open `.gitignore` and add these lines at the bottom:
```
.env
threats.db
venv/

Step 4: Create the AI analyzer

Right-click on the services folder → New Folder → name it ai. Then right-click the new ai folder:

  • Create __init__.py (leave empty)
  • Create analyzer.py with this code:
python
import anthropic
import json
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

# Load the API key from .env file
load_dotenv()


def analyze_threats(threats):
    """
    Send a batch of threats to Claude and get back a structured analysis
    including summaries, categories, and risk assessment.
    """
    
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    
    # Build a prompt with the threat data
    threat_text = ""
    for t in threats:
        threat_text += f"""
CVE ID: {t['id']}
Source: {t['source']}
Severity: {t['severity']} (Score: {t['score']})
Description: {t['description'][:300]}
---
"""
    
    message = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
        max_tokens=2000,
        messages=[
            {
                "role": "user",
                "content": f"""You are a cybersecurity analyst. Analyze these vulnerabilities and return a JSON response with this exact structure:

{{
    "summary": "A 2-3 sentence overview of the current threat landscape based on these vulnerabilities",
    "threats": [
        {{
            "id": "CVE-XXXX-XXXXX",
            "category": "one of: Remote Code Execution, Privilege Escalation, Data Exposure, Denial of Service, Authentication Bypass, Injection, Other",
            "risk_level": "CRITICAL, HIGH, MEDIUM, or LOW",
            "plain_summary": "A one-sentence explanation a non-technical person could understand",
            "action": "A one-sentence recommended action"
        }}
    ],
    "top_priority": "The CVE ID that should be addressed first and why in one sentence"
}}

Here are the vulnerabilities to analyze:

{threat_text}

Return ONLY valid JSON, no other text."""
            }
        ]
    )
    
    # Parse the JSON response
    response_text = message.content[0].text
    
    try:
        analysis = json.loads(response_text)
    except json.JSONDecodeError:
        # Sometimes the response has markdown code blocks around it
        cleaned = response_text.strip().strip("```json").strip("```").strip()
        analysis = json.loads(cleaned)
    
    return analysis


if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Pull threats from the database and analyze them
    from backend.app.models import Threat, get_session
    
    session = get_session()
    threats = session.query(Threat).limit(5).all()
    session.close()
    
    # Convert database objects to dictionaries
    threat_list = [
        {
            "id": t.id,
            "source": t.source,
            "description": t.description,
            "severity": t.severity,
            "score": t.score,
        }
        for t in threats
    ]
    
    print("Sending threats to Claude for analysis...\n")
    analysis = analyze_threats(threat_list)
    
    print("=== THREAT LANDSCAPE SUMMARY ===")
    print(analysis["summary"])
    print()
    
    print("=== INDIVIDUAL THREAT ANALYSIS ===")
    for t in analysis["threats"]:
        print(f"\n{t['id']}")
        print(f"  Category:    {t['category']}")
        print(f"  Risk Level:  {t['risk_level']}")
        print(f"  Summary:     {t['plain_summary']}")
        print(f"  Action:      {t['action']}")
    
    print(f"\n=== TOP PRIORITY ===")
    print(analysis["top_priority"])

Step 5: Run it

bash
python -m backend.app.services.ai.analyzer

This will pull 5 threats from your database, send them to Claude, and print an AI-generated security briefing. Let me know what you see!

(venv) jasimalnajjar@MacBookPro threat-intel-dashboard % python -m backend.app.services.ai.analyzer Sending threats to Claude for analysis... Traceback (most recent call last): File "/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.10/lib/python3.10/runpy.py", line 196, in _run_module_as_main return _run_code(code, main_globals, None, File "/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.10/lib/python3.10/runpy.py", line 86, in _run_code exec(code, run_globals) File "/Users/jasimalnajjar/threat-intel-dashboard/backend/app/services/ai/analyzer.py", line 94, in <module> analysis = analyze_threats(threat_list) File "/Users/jasimalnajjar/threat-intel-dashboard/backend/app/services/ai/analyzer.py", line 29, in analyze_threats message = client.messages.create( File "/Users/jasimalnajjar/threat-intel-dashboard/venv/lib/python3.10/site-packages/anthropic/_utils/_utils.py", line 282, in wrapper return func(*args, **kwargs) File "/Users/jasimalnajjar/threat-intel-dashboard/venv/lib/python3.10/site-packages/anthropic/resources/messages/messages.py", line 996, in create return self._post( File "/Users/jasimalnajjar/threat-intel-dashboard/venv/lib/python3.10/site-packages/anthropic/_base_client.py", line 1368, in post return cast(ResponseT, self.request(cast_to, opts, stream=stream, stream_cls=stream_cls)) File "/Users/jasimalnajjar/threat-intel-dashboard/venv/lib/python3.10/site-packages/anthropic/_base_client.py", line 1062, in request request = self._build_request(options, retries_taken=retries_taken) File "/Users/jasimalnajjar/threat-intel-dashboard/venv/lib/python3.10/site-packages/anthropic/_base_client.py", line 521, in _build_request headers = self._build_headers(options, retries_taken=retries_taken) File "/Users/jasimalnajjar/threat-intel-dashboard/venv/lib/python3.10/site-packages/anthropic/_base_client.py", line 451, in _build_headers self._validate_headers(headers_dict, custom_headers) File "/Users/jasimalnajjar/threat-intel-dashboard/venv/lib/python3.10/site-packages/anthropic/_client.py", line 196, in _validate_headers raise TypeError( TypeError: "Could not resolve authentication method. Expected either api_key or auth_token to be set. Or for one of the X-Api-Key or Authorization headers to be explicitly omitted"

21:46

The API key isn't being picked up. Let's check your .env file is in the right place:

bash
cat .env
```

Make sure it shows something like:
```
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-api03-xxxxx

If the file looks correct, the issue might be that dotenv is looking in the wrong directory. Let's fix it by updating the path in analyzer.py. Open the file in VS Code and replace this line:

python
load_dotenv()

With:

python
import pathlib
load_dotenv(pathlib.Path(__file__).resolve().parents[4] / ".env")

This tells it exactly where to find the .env file. Save and run again:

bash
python -m backend.app.services.ai.analyzer

Let me know what happens!

(venv) jasimalnajjar@MacBookPro threat-intel-dashboard % cat .env cat: .env: No such file or directory

21:47

bash

Replace your-key-here with your actual API key from console.anthropic.com. It should start with sk-ant-.

Then confirm it's there:

bash
cat .env

Once you see your key printed, run the analyzer again:

bash
python -m backend.app.services.ai.analyzer

looks like it was created maybe under ingestion?

This will show where it ended up. Paste what you see and we'll move it to the right place.

(venv) jasimalnajjar@MacBookPro threat-intel-dashboard % find ~ -name ".env" 2>/dev/null | grep threat /Users/jasimalnajjar/threat-intel-dashboard/backend/app/services/ingestion/.env

bash

git commit -m "Phase 2: Add AI threat analysis with Claude API"

bash
cat .gitignore
```

If you don't see `.env` listed in there, open `.gitignore` in VS Code and add these lines at the bottom:
```
