threats.db
venv/

Save it, then do the git push commands above. Let me know when you're ready for Phase 3 — the backend API!

ready

21:53

Phase 3 — Backend API

The concept

Right now everything runs from the terminal. We need an API — think of it as a waiter between your frontend (dashboard) and your backend (database + AI). The frontend will ask the API "give me all threats" or "analyze these threats" and the API sends back the data as JSON.

We'll use FastAPI — it's a Python framework that makes building APIs very simple and it auto-generates documentation you can test in your browser.

Step 1: Install dependencies

bash
pip install fastapi uvicorn

FastAPI is the framework. Uvicorn is the server that runs it (like how a car engine needs a car to be useful).

Step 2: Create the API

Right-click on the app folder → New File → name it main.py:

python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Query
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware
from backend.app.models import Threat, get_session
from backend.app.services.ai.analyzer import analyze_threats

app = FastAPI(
    title="Threat Intelligence Dashboard API",
    description="API for aggregating and analyzing cyber threat data",
    version="1.0.0"
)

# Allow the frontend to talk to this API
app.add_middleware(
    CORSMiddleware,
    allow_origins=["*"],
    allow_methods=["*"],
    allow_headers=["*"],
)


@app.get("/")
def home():
    """Health check — just confirms the API is running."""
    return {"status": "online", "message": "Threat Intel Dashboard API"}


@app.get("/threats")
def get_threats(
    source: str = Query(None, description="Filter by source: NVD or CISA KEV"),
    severity: str = Query(None, description="Filter by severity: CRITICAL, HIGH, MEDIUM, LOW"),
    limit: int = Query(20, description="Number of results to return"),
):
    """
    Get threats from the database with optional filters.
    This is what the dashboard will call to populate the threat list.
    """
    session = get_session()
    query = session.query(Threat)

    if source:
        query = query.filter(Threat.source == source)
    if severity:
        query = query.filter(Threat.severity == severity.upper())

    threats = query.limit(limit).all()
    session.close()

    return [
        {
            "id": t.id,
            "source": t.source,
            "description": t.description,
            "severity": t.severity,
            "score": t.score,
            "published": t.published,
            "vendor": t.vendor,
            "product": t.product,
        }
        for t in threats
    ]


@app.get("/threats/stats")
def get_stats():
    """
    Get summary statistics for the dashboard cards.
    Things like total threats, breakdown by severity, etc.
    """
    session = get_session()

    total = session.query(Threat).count()

    severity_counts = {}
    for level in ["CRITICAL", "HIGH", "MEDIUM", "LOW"]:
        count = session.query(Threat).filter(Threat.severity == level).count()
        if count > 0:
            severity_counts[level] = count

    source_counts = {}
    for source in ["NVD", "CISA KEV"]:
        count = session.query(Threat).filter(Threat.source == source).count()
        if count > 0:
            source_counts[source] = count

    session.close()

    return {
        "total_threats": total,
        "by_severity": severity_counts,
        "by_source": source_counts,
    }


@app.get("/analyze")
def run_analysis(limit: int = Query(5, description="Number of threats to analyze")):
    """
    Send threats to Claude for AI analysis.
    Returns a structured security briefing.
    """
    session = get_session()
    threats = session.query(Threat).limit(limit).all()
    session.close()

    threat_list = [
        {
            "id": t.id,
            "source": t.source,
            "description": t.description,
            "severity": t.severity,
            "score": t.score,
        }
        for t in threats
    ]

    analysis = analyze_threats(threat_list)
    return analysis

Step 3: Run the API

bash
python -m uvicorn backend.app.main:app --reload

You should see something like Uvicorn running on http://127.0.0.1:8000.

Step 4: Test it in your browser

Open these URLs one at a time:

  • http://127.0.0.1:8000 → should show the health check message
  • http://127.0.0.1:8000/threats → should show your stored threats as JSON
  • http://127.0.0.1:8000/threats/stats → should show severity/source counts
  • http://127.0.0.1:8000/docs → this is the auto-generated API docs where you can test everything interactively

Try each one and let me know what you see! The /docs page is especially cool — it lets you click buttons to test each endpoint.

ywp works

21:57

Excellent! You now have a working API serving real threat data. Let's push and move to the final phase — the dashboard:

bash
git add .
git commit -m "Phase 3: Add FastAPI backend with threats, stats, and analysis endpoints"
git push

Phase 4 — The Dashboard UI

The concept

This is the visual layer — a React app that calls your API and displays everything in a professional dashboard. We'll build it with charts, filters, and an AI analysis panel.

Step 1: Create the React app

Open a new terminal in VS Code (Terminal → New Terminal) — keep the API running in the other one. Then:

bash
npx create-react-app frontend
cd frontend
npm install axios recharts

axios is for making API calls from React. recharts is for the charts. This might take a minute or two.

Step 2: Replace the default app

Open frontend/src/App.js in VS Code, delete everything in it, and paste this:

javascript
import React, { useState, useEffect } from "react";
import axios from "axios";
import {
  BarChart, Bar, XAxis, YAxis, CartesianGrid, Tooltip,
  PieChart, Pie, Cell, ResponsiveContainer, Legend
} from "recharts";
import "./App.css";

const API_URL = "http://127.0.0.1:8000";
const COLORS = {
  CRITICAL: "#e74c3c",
  HIGH: "#e67e22",
  MEDIUM: "#f1c40f",
  LOW: "#2ecc71",
  Unknown: "#95a5a6"
};

function App() {
  const [threats, setThreats] = useState([]);
  const [stats, setStats] = useState(null);
  const [analysis, setAnalysis] = useState(null);
  const [loading, setLoading] = useState(true);
  const [analyzing, setAnalyzing] = useState(false);
  const [filter, setFilter] = useState({ source: "", severity: "" });

  // Fetch threats and stats when the page loads
  useEffect(() => {
    fetchData();
  }, []);

  async function fetchData() {
    try {
      const [threatRes, statsRes] = await Promise.all([
        axios.get(`${API_URL}/threats?limit=50`),
        axios.get(`${API_URL}/threats/stats`),
      ]);
      setThreats(threatRes.data);
      setStats(statsRes.data);
      setLoading(false);
    } catch (error) {
      console.error("Error fetching data:", error);
      setLoading(false);
    }
  }

  async function runAnalysis() {
    setAnalyzing(true);
    try {
      const res = await axios.get(`${API_URL}/analyze?limit=5`);
      setAnalysis(res.data);
    } catch (error) {
      console.error("Error running analysis:", error);
    }
    setAnalyzing(false);
  }

  // Apply filters
  const filteredThreats = threats.filter((t) => {
    if (filter.source && t.source !== filter.source) return false;
    if (filter.severity && t.severity !== filter.severity) return false;
    return true;
  });

  if (loading) return <div className="loading">Loading threat data...</div>;

  // Prepare chart data
  const severityData = stats?.by_severity
    ? Object.entries(stats.by_severity).map(([name, value]) => ({ name, value }))
    : [];

  const sourceData = stats?.by_source
    ? Object.entries(stats.by_source).map(([name, value]) => ({ name, value }))
    : [];

  return (
    <div className="app">
      <header className="header">
        <h1>Threat Intelligence Dashboard</h1>
        <p>Real-time cyber threat monitoring and AI-powered analysis</p>
      </header>

      {/* Stats Cards */}
      <div className="stats-grid">
        <div className="stat-card total">
          <h3>Total Threats</h3>
          <span className="stat-number">{stats?.total_threats || 0}</span>
        </div>
        {Object.entries(stats?.by_severity || {}).map(([level, count]) => (
          <div className="stat-card" key={level} style={{ borderLeftColor: COLORS[level] }}>
            <h3>{level}</h3>
            <span className="stat-number">{count}</span>
          </div>
        ))}
      </div>

      {/* Charts */}
      <div className="charts-grid">
        <div className="chart-card">
          <h3>Threats by Severity</h3>
          <ResponsiveContainer width="100%" height={250}>
            <BarChart data={severityData}>
              <CartesianGrid strokeDasharray="3 3" />
              <XAxis dataKey="name" />
              <YAxis />
              <Tooltip />
              <Bar dataKey="value">
                {severityData.map((entry) => (
                  <Cell key={entry.name} fill={COLORS[entry.name] || COLORS.Unknown} />
                ))}
              </Bar>
            </BarChart>
          </ResponsiveContainer>
        </div>

        <div className="chart-card">
          <h3>Threats by Source</h3>
          <ResponsiveContainer width="100%" height={250}>
            <PieChart>
              <Pie data={sourceData} dataKey="value" nameKey="name" cx="50%" cy="50%" outerRadius={80} label>
                {sourceData.map((_, i) => (
                  <Cell key={i} fill={["#3498db", "#9b59b6", "#1abc9c"][i]} />
                ))}
              </Pie>
              <Tooltip />
              <Legend />
            </PieChart>
          </ResponsiveContainer>
        </div>
      </div>

      {/* AI Analysis */}
      <div className="analysis-section">
        <div className="analysis-header">
          <h2>AI Threat Analysis</h2>
          <button onClick={runAnalysis} disabled={analyzing} className="analyze-btn">
            {analyzing ? "Analyzing..." : "Run AI Analysis"}
          </button>
        </div>

        {analysis && (
          <div className="analysis-results">
            <div className="summary-card">
              <h3>Threat Landscape Summary</h3>
              <p>{analysis.summary}</p>
            </div>

            <div className="priority-card">
              <h3>Top Priority</h3>
              <p>{analysis.top_priority}</p>
            </div>

            <div className="analysis-grid">
              {analysis.threats?.map((t) => (
                <div className="analysis-item" key={t.id}>
                  <div className="analysis-item-header">
                    <span className="cve-id">{t.id}</span>
                    <span className={`risk-badge ${t.risk_level?.toLowerCase()}`}>
                      {t.risk_level}
                    </span>
                  </div>
                  <p className="category">{t.category}</p>
                  <p className="plain-summary">{t.plain_summary}</p>
                  <p className="action"><strong>Action:</strong> {t.action}</p>
                </div>
              ))}
            </div>
          </div>
        )}
      </div>

      {/* Threat Table */}
      <div className="table-section">
        <div className="table-header">
          <h2>Threat Feed</h2>
          <div className="filters">
            <select value={filter.source} onChange={(e) => setFilter({ ...filter, source: e.target.value })}>
              <option value="">All Sources</option>
              <option value="NVD">NVD</option>
              <option value="CISA KEV">CISA KEV</option>
            </select>
            <select value={filter.severity} onChange={(e) => setFilter({ ...filter, severity: e.target.value })}>
              <option value="">All Severities</option>
              <option value="CRITICAL">Critical</option>
              <option value="HIGH">High</option>
              <option value="MEDIUM">Medium</option>
              <option value="LOW">Low</option>
            </select>
          </div>
        </div>

        <table className="threat-table">
          <thead>
            <tr>
              <th>CVE ID</th>
              <th>Severity</th>
              <th>Score</th>
              <th>Source</th>
              <th>Description</th>
            </tr>
          </thead>
          <tbody>
            {filteredThreats.map((t) => (
              <tr key={t.id}>
                <td className="cve-id">{t.id}</td>
                <td>
                  <span className={`severity-badge ${t.severity?.toLowerCase()}`}>
                    {t.severity}
                  </span>
                </td>
                <td>{t.score || "N/A"}</td>
                <td>{t.source}</td>
                <td className="description-cell">{t.description?.slice(0, 150)}...</td>
              </tr>
            ))}
          </tbody>
        </table>
      </div>
    </div>
  );
}

export default App;

Step 3: Replace the CSS

Open frontend/src/App.css, delete everything, and paste this:

css
* {
  margin: 0;
  padding: 0;
  box-sizing: border-box;
}

body {
  font-family: -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, "Segoe UI", Roboto, sans-serif;
  background: #0a0e17;
  color: #e0e0e0;
}

.app {
  max-width: 1400px;
  margin: 0 auto;
  padding: 20px;
}

.loading {
  display: flex;
  justify-content: center;
  align-items: center;
  height: 100vh;
  font-size: 1.5rem;
  color: #3498db;
}

/* Header */
.header {
  text-align: center;
  padding: 30px 0;
  border-bottom: 1px solid #1a2035;
  margin-bottom: 30px;
}

.header h1 {
  font-size: 2rem;
  color: #3498db;
  margin-bottom: 8px;
}

.header p {
  color: #7f8c8d;
  font-size: 1rem;
}

/* Stats Cards */
.stats-grid {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(150px, 1fr));
  gap: 15px;
  margin-bottom: 30px;
}

.stat-card {
  background: #111827;
  border-radius: 10px;
  padding: 20px;
  border-left: 4px solid #3498db;
  text-align: center;
}

.stat-card.total {
  border-left-color: #3498db;
}

.stat-card h3 {
  font-size: 0.85rem;
  color: #7f8c8d;
  text-transform: uppercase;
  margin-bottom: 8px;
}

.stat-number {
  font-size: 2rem;
  font-weight: 700;
  color: #fff;
}

/* Charts */
.charts-grid {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr;
  gap: 20px;
  margin-bottom: 30px;
}

.chart-card {
  background: #111827;
  border-radius: 10px;
  padding: 20px;
}

.chart-card h3 {
  margin-bottom: 15px;
  color: #bdc3c7;
  font-size: 1rem;
}

/* AI Analysis */
.analysis-section {
  margin-bottom: 30px;
}

.analysis-header {
  display: flex;
  justify-content: space-between;
  align-items: center;
  margin-bottom: 20px;
}

.analysis-header h2 {
  color: #3498db;
}

.analyze-btn {
  background: #3498db;
  color: white;
  border: none;
  padding: 10px 24px;
  border-radius: 6px;
  font-size: 1rem;
  cursor: pointer;
  transition: background 0.2s;
}

.analyze-btn:hover {
  background: #2980b9;
}

.analyze-btn:disabled {
  background: #555;
  cursor: not-allowed;
}

.summary-card, .priority-card {
  background: #111827;
  border-radius: 10px;
  padding: 20px;
  margin-bottom: 15px;
}

.summary-card h3, .priority-card h3 {
  color: #3498db;
  margin-bottom: 10px;
}

.priority-card {
  border-left: 4px solid #e74c3c;
}

.analysis-grid {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(300px, 1fr));
  gap: 15px;
}

.analysis-item {
  background: #111827;
  border-radius: 10px;
  padding: 15px;
}

.analysis-item-header {
  display: flex;
  justify-content: space-between;
  align-items: center;
  margin-bottom: 10px;
}

.cve-id {
  font-family: monospace;
  color: #3498db;
  font-weight: 600;
}

.risk-badge, .severity-badge {
  padding: 3px 10px;
  border-radius: 12px;
  font-size: 0.75rem;
  font-weight: 600;
  text-transform: uppercase;
}

.risk-badge.critical, .severity-badge.critical { background: #e74c3c33; color: #e74c3c; }
.risk-badge.high, .severity-badge.high { background: #e67e2233; color: #e67e22; }
.risk-badge.medium, .severity-badge.medium { background: #f1c40f33; color: #f1c40f; }
.risk-badge.low, .severity-badge.low { background: #2ecc7133; color: #2ecc71; }

.category {
  color: #9b59b6;
  font-size: 0.85rem;
  margin-bottom: 8px;
}

.plain-summary {
  color: #bdc3c7;
  font-size: 0.9rem;
  margin-bottom: 8px;
}

.action {
  font-size: 0.85rem;
  color: #7f8c8d;
}

/* Threat Table */
.table-section {
  margin-bottom: 30px;
}

.table-header {
  display: flex;
  justify-content: space-between;
  align-items: center;
  margin-bottom: 15px;
}

.table-header h2 {
  color: #3498db;
}

.filters {
  display: flex;
  gap: 10px;
}

.filters select {
  background: #111827;
  color: #e0e0e0;
  border: 1px solid #1a2035;
  padding: 8px 12px;
  border-radius: 6px;
  font-size: 0.9rem;
}

.threat-table {
  width: 100%;
  border-collapse: collapse;
  background: #111827;
  border-radius: 10px;
  overflow: hidden;
}

.threat-table th {
  background: #1a2035;
  padding: 12px 15px;
  text-align: left;
  color: #7f8c8d;
  font-size: 0.85rem;
  text-transform: uppercase;
}

.threat-table td {
  padding: 12px 15px;
  border-bottom: 1px solid #1a2035;
  font-size: 0.9rem;
}

.threat-table tr:hover {
  background: #1a203550;
}

.description-cell {
  max-width: 400px;
  color: #95a5a6;
}

Step 4: Run the dashboard

Make sure your API is still running in the other terminal, then:

bash
cd frontend
npm start

This will open http://localhost:3000 in your browser automatically. You should see your dashboard with charts, stats cards, a threat table, and the AI analysis button.

Let me know what you see!

can anything else be used apart from axios as this was recently hit by a supply chain

22:02

Great security awareness — that's exactly the kind of thinking this project should showcase. Yes, you can use fetch, which is built right into JavaScript. No package to install, no supply chain risk.

Replace this line in your App.js:

javascript
import axios from "axios";

With nothing — just delete it. Then find and replace the three axios calls. Here's what to change:

First call — replace:

javascript
const [threatRes, statsRes] = await Promise.all([
  axios.get(`${API_URL}/threats?limit=50`),
  axios.get(`${API_URL}/threats/stats`),
]);
setThreats(threatRes.data);
setStats(statsRes.data);

With:

javascript
const [threatRes, statsRes] = await Promise.all([
  fetch(`${API_URL}/threats?limit=50`).then(res => res.json()),
  fetch(`${API_URL}/threats/stats`).then(res => res.json()),
]);
setThreats(threatRes);
setStats(statsRes);

Second call — replace:

javascript
const res = await axios.get(`${API_URL}/analyze?limit=5`);
setAnalysis(res.data);

With:

javascript
const res = await fetch(`${API_URL}/analyze?limit=5`);
const data = await res.json();
setAnalysis(data);

Then you can also uninstall axios:

bash
npm uninstall axios

Save and your dashboard should work exactly the same, with zero third-party HTTP dependencies. Let me know how it goes!

venv) jasimalnajjar@MacBookPro frontend % npm start > frontend@0.1.0 start > react-scripts start sh: react-scripts: command not found

22:10

The dependencies didn't install properly. Run this:

bash
npm install

Wait for it to finish, then try again:

bash
npm start

still didn't wokr

22:11

Try deleting node_modules and reinstalling:

bash
rm -rf node_modules package-lock.json
npm install
npm start

If that still doesn't work, paste the error message you see.

(venv) jasimalnajjar@MacBookPro frontend % rm -rf node_modules package-lock.json (venv) jasimalnajjar@MacBookPro frontend % npm install added 70 packages, and audited 71 packages in 3s 3 packages are looking for funding run npm fund for details found 0 vulnerabilities (venv) jasimalnajjar@MacBookPro frontend % npm start > frontend@0.1.0 start > react-scripts start sh: react-scripts: command not found

Is it because I unistalled axios due to supply chain?

22:12

No, uninstalling axios wouldn't cause this. The issue is that react-scripts isn't installed. Let's check:

bash
cat package.json

Paste what you see — I want to check what dependencies are listed.

(venv) jasimalnajjar@MacBookPro frontend % cat package.json { "name": "frontend", "version": "0.1.0", "private": true, "dependencies": { "@testing-library/dom": "^10.4.1", "@testing-library/jest-dom": "^6.9.1", "@testing-library/react": "^16.3.2", "@testing-library/user-event": "^13.5.0", "react": "^19.2.5", "react-dom": "^19.2.5", "react-scripts": "^0.0.0", "recharts": "^3.8.1", "web-vitals": "^2.1.4" }, "scripts": { "start": "react-scripts start", "build": "react-scripts build", "test": "react-scripts test", "eject": "react-scripts eject" }, "eslintConfig": { "extends": [ "react-app", "react-app/jest" ] }, "browserslist": { "production": [ ">0.2%", "not dead", "not op_mini all" ], "development": [ "last 1 chrome version", "last 1 firefox version", "last 1 safari version" ] } }

22:13

There it is — react-scripts is version "^0.0.0" which is basically nothing. Let's fix it:

bash
npm install react-scripts@5 --save

Then:

bash
npm start

out of curosity I'm already running something on my host file for another project will this override it?

22:14

No, it won't override anything. Each app runs on a different port number. Think of your computer as a building — each port is a different room:

  • Your other project is probably on port 3000 or another port
  • React will try port 3000 first, but if it's taken, it'll ask you: "Port 3000 is in use, would you like to use port 3001 instead?" — just say yes

Your API is on port 8000, so no conflicts there either.

Go ahead and run npm start — if it detects a conflict it'll prompt you before doing anything.

perfect can see dashboard

22:15

That's amazing, Jas! You just built a full-stack AI-powered threat intelligence dashboard from scratch. Let's push everything:

bash
cd ~/threat-intel-dashboard/threat-intel-dashboard
git add .
git commit -m "Phase 4: Add React dashboard with charts, filters, and AI analysis"
git push

Let's take stock of what you've built — this is genuinely impressive for a portfolio project:

  • Data pipeline pulling from two live OSINT feeds (NVD + CISA)
  • Database storing and deduplicating threat data
  • AI analysis using Claude to categorize, summarize, and prioritize threats
  • REST API with filtering, stats, and analysis endpoints
  • Dashboard UI with charts, severity cards, threat table, and AI briefing panel

Next steps to polish it for GitHub

When you're ready, we can work on any of these:

  1. README — a professional README with screenshots, architecture diagram, and setup instructions. This is what recruiters see first.
  2. Docker Compose — so anyone can run the whole thing with one command
  3. More data sources — add AlienVault OTX or Abuse.ch feeds
  4. Semantic search — let users search threats in natural language using embeddings
  5. Auto-refresh — schedule the ingestion to run automatically

Which would you like to tackle next?

lets start with point 1

22:17

Let's create a strong README. First, take a screenshot of your dashboard and save it somewhere — we'll need it.

In the meantime, let me build the README for you.

Check the skill file for creating markdown

That skill is for Word docs — the README is just a markdown file. Let me create it directly.

Created a file, read a file

Here's your README. To add it to your project:

  1. Download the file above
  2. Replace the existing README.md in your project root with this one
  3. Take a screenshot of your dashboard and save it as docs/screenshot.png in your project — then uncomment the screenshot line near the top of the README
  4. Push it:
bash
mkdir docs
# move your screenshot into docs/screenshot.png
git add .
git commit -m "Add professional README with architecture diagram and setup guide"
git push

One thing that'll really make this pop — once you have the screenshot in there, that's the first thing people see when they land on your repo. If you want, you can also record a short GIF showing the AI analysis button in action.

Ready to tackle any of the other improvements, or would you like to tweak the README first?

Readme
Document · MD 

how do I do this part after saving? Take a screenshot of your dashboard and save it as docs/screenshot.png in your project — then uncomment the screenshot line near the top of the README

22:25

Step 1: Take the screenshot

  1. Make sure both your API and frontend are running
  2. Open the dashboard in your browser at http://localhost:3000
  3. Press Cmd + Shift + 3 to screenshot your full screen, or Cmd + Shift + 4 to select just the dashboard area
  4. The screenshot saves to your Desktop by default

Step 2: Move it into your project

bash
cd ~/threat-intel-dashboard/threat-intel-dashboard
mkdir docs
mv ~/Desktop/Screenshot*.png docs/screenshot.png
```

If the file on your Desktop has a different name, you can check with `ls ~/Desktop/Screen*` and adjust.

### Step 3: Uncomment the line in README.md

Open `README.md` in VS Code and find this line near the top:
```
<!-- ![Dashboard Screenshot](docs/screenshot.png) -->
```

Change it to:
```
![Dashboard Screenshot](docs/screenshot.png)

Just remove the <!-- at the start and --> at the end. Save the file.

Step 4: Push

bash
git add .
git commit -m "Add dashboard screenshot to README"
git push

Now when anyone visits your repo on GitHub, they'll see the dashboard screenshot right at the top. Let me know when it's up!

screenshot isn't showing

22:28

Let's check if the file is in the right place:

bash
ls docs/

And check what the file is actually named:

bash
ls ~/Desktop/Screen*

Paste both outputs — the filename probably didn't match the mv command exactly.

currently my images are in desktop how can I move them to the docs folder?

22:33

First let's see the exact filenames:

bash
ls ~/Desktop/Screen*

Paste what you see and I'll give you the exact command to move them.

(venv) jasimalnajjar@MacBookPro Desktop % ls ~/Desktop/Screen* zsh: no matches found: /Users/jasimalnajjar/Desktop/Screen*

22:33

The screenshots might be saving somewhere else on your Mac. Try:

bash
ls ~/Desktop/*.png

If nothing shows, try:

bash
find ~ -name "Screenshot*" -maxdepth 3 2>/dev/null

Paste what comes up and we'll find them.

it's saved under desktop

(venv) jasimalnajjar@MacBookPro Desktop % ls Blumi Maarif Creative Arts High Work Sample 2.png Naya Creative Arts High Work Sample.png Python Creative Arts Low Work Sample 2.png Resume Creative Arts Low Work Sample.png Weave Creative Arts Mid Work Sample 2.png docs:screenshot1.png Creative Arts Mid Work Sample.png docs:screenshot2.png

22:34

There they are — but the filenames have colons (docs:screenshot1.png) which macOS treats differently. Let's copy them to your project:

bash
cp ~/Desktop/docs:screenshot1.png ~/threat-intel-dashboard/threat-intel-dashboard/docs/screenshot1.png
cp ~/Desktop/docs:screenshot2.png ~/threat-intel-dashboard/threat-intel-dashboard/docs/screenshot2.png
```

If that doesn't work because of the colons, try dragging and dropping instead:

1. Open **Finder**
2. Navigate to your Desktop and find the two screenshot files
3. Open another Finder window and go to `threat-intel-dashboard/threat-intel-dashboard/docs/`
4. Drag the files into the `docs` folder
5. Rename them to `screenshot1.png` and `screenshot2.png`

Then update the README in VS Code. Find:
```
<!-- ![Dashboard Screenshot](docs/screenshot.png) -->
```

Replace with:
```
![Dashboard Screenshot](docs/screenshot1.png)
![Dashboard Screenshot](docs/screenshot2.png)

Save and push:

bash
cd ~/threat-intel-dashboard/threat-intel-dashboard
git add .
git commit -m "Add dashboard screenshots to README"
git push

Let me know if the screenshots show up on GitHub!

still not working

22:40

The broken image icons mean GitHub can't find the files. Let's check if they're actually in the docs folder:

bash
cd ~/threat-intel-dashboard/threat-intel-dashboard
ls docs/

Paste what you see. Also check what the README says:

bash
grep "screenshot" README.md

The image paths in the README need to match the exact filenames in the docs folder.

(venv) jasimalnajjar@MacBookPro docs % ls docs:screenshot1.png docs:screenshot2.png (venv) jasimalnajjar@MacBookPro docs % grep "screenshot" README.md grep: README.md: No such file or directory (venv) jasimalnajjar@MacBookPro docs % ls docs:screenshot1.png docs:screenshot2.png (venv) jasimalnajjar@MacBookPro docs % ls docs/ ls: docs/: No such file or directory (venv) jasimalnajjar@MacBookPro docs %

22:41

The problem is the filenames have docs: in them. Let's rename them:

bash
cd ~/threat-intel-dashboard/threat-intel-dashboard/docs
mv 'docs:screenshot1.png' screenshot1.png
mv 'docs:screenshot2.png' screenshot2.png

Confirm they renamed:

bash
ls

You should see screenshot1.png and screenshot2.png. Then push:

bash
cd ~/threat-intel-dashboard/threat-intel-dashboard
git add .
git commit -m "Fix screenshot filenames"
git push

Check GitHub again — the images should load now.

still not working is this correct


![Dashboard Screenshot](screenshot1.png)
![Dashboard Screenshot](screenshot2.png)
22:49

Since the screenshots are inside the docs folder, you need the path to include that. Change it to:

![Dashboard Screenshot](docs/screenshot1.png)
![Dashboard Screenshot](docs/screenshot2.png)

Save the README, then push:

bash
git add .
git commit -m "Fix screenshot paths in README"
git push

lets move to Docker Compose — so anyone can run the whole thing with one command

22:51

Docker Compose Setup

The concept

Docker is like a shipping container for your app. It packages your code, dependencies, and settings into a container that runs exactly the same on anyone's machine. Docker Compose lets you define multiple containers (backend + frontend) and start them all with one command.

Step 1: Install Docker

  1. Go to docker.com/products/docker-desktop
  2. Download Docker Desktop for Mac
  3. Install and open it
  4. Wait until the Docker icon in your menu bar shows it's running (the whale icon stops animating)

Confirm it's working:

bash
docker --version

Let me know once Docker is installed and running, and I'll give you the next steps to create the Dockerfiles and docker-compose.yml.

while I wait for the install - I don't like the architecture diagram in readme file. How can I change this to a draw.io

22:52

Good call — a proper diagram will look much more professional than ASCII art. Here's how:

Step 1: Create the diagram

  1. Go to app.diagrams.net (that's draw.io)
  2. Choose Create New Diagram
  3. Save to Device

Step 2: Build the architecture

Create boxes and arrows for this flow. Here's what to include:

Top layer — Frontend:

  • Box: "React Dashboard" with sub-items: Charts, Threat Table, AI Analysis Panel

Middle layer — Backend:

  • Box: "FastAPI Backend" with sub-items: /threats, /threats/stats, /analyze

Bottom layer — two boxes side by side:

  • Box: "SQLite Database (threats.db)"
  • Box: "Claude AI API (Anthropic)"

Bottom layer — Data Sources:

  • Box: "NVD (NIST)"
  • Box: "CISA KEV"
  • Both with arrows pointing up into the database

Use arrows to show the data flow between each layer. A dark background with blue/purple accent colors will match your dashboard's look.

Step 3: Export as PNG

Once your diagram looks good, go to File → Export as → PNG. Set the zoom to 2x for a crisp image.

Step 4: Add it to your project

Save the PNG to your docs folder as architecture.png. Then update the README in VS Code — find the ASCII architecture diagram (the big block between the triple backticks) and replace it with:

![Architecture Diagram](docs/architecture.png)

Delete the entire ASCII block that starts with ┌─── and ends with └───.

Step 5: Push

bash
cd ~/threat-intel-dashboard/threat-intel-dashboard
git add .
git commit -m "Replace ASCII architecture with draw.io diagram"
git push

Want me to be more specific about the layout or colors for the diagram?

can you draw it on draw.io?

22:53

I can't directly use draw.io, but I can create an SVG diagram for you that you can either use as-is or import into draw.io to edit further. Let me make one that matches your dashboard's dark theme.

V

visualize

V

visualize show_widget

Here's your architecture diagram. You can save this as an image by right-clicking it and selecting "Save image as..." — then put it in your docs folder as architecture.png.

Alternatively, if you want to edit it further in draw.io, I can export it as a draw.io XML file that you can import and customize. Want me to do that, or does this version work for you?


Claude is AI and can make mistakes. Please double-check responses.

Readme · MD

1
# 🛡️ Threat Intelligence Dashboard
2
 
3
An AI-powered threat intelligence platform that aggregates OSINT feeds, uses Claude AI to analyze and prioritize vulnerabilities, and presents actionable insights through a real-time dashboard.
4
 
5
![Python](https://img.shields.io/badge/Python-3.10+-blue?logo=python&logoColor=white)
6
![React](https://img.shields.io/badge/React-19-61dafb?logo=react&logoColor=white)
7
![FastAPI](https://img.shields.io/badge/FastAPI-0.100+-009688?logo=fastapi&logoColor=white)
8
![Claude AI](https://img.shields.io/badge/Claude_AI-Anthropic-6B4FBB)
9
![License](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-MIT-green)
10
 
11
<!-- Add your screenshot here -->
12
<!-- ![Dashboard Screenshot](docs/screenshot.png) -->
13
 
14
---
15
 
16
## Overview
17
 
18
Security teams are overwhelmed by the volume of vulnerability disclosures. This dashboard solves that by automatically collecting threat data from multiple OSINT sources, using AI to cluster, categorize, and summarize threats, and surfacing what matters most in a clean, interactive UI.
19
 
20
### What It Does
21
 
22
- **Aggregates** vulnerability data from NVD and CISA KEV feeds in real time
23
- **Stores** and deduplicates threats in a local SQLite database
24
- **Analyzes** threats using Claude AI — generating plain-English summaries, risk assessments, MITRE ATT&CK categorization, and prioritized action items
25
- **Visualizes** the threat landscape with severity breakdowns, source distribution charts, and a filterable threat feed
26
 
27
---
28
 
29
## Architecture
30
 
31
```
32
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
33
│ React Frontend │
34
│ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌───────────────────┐ │
35
│ │ Severity │ │ Source │ │ Threat Table │ │
36
│ │ Charts │ │ Charts │ │ with Filters │ │
37
│ └──────────┘ └──────────┘ └───────────────────┘ │
38
│ ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
39
│ │ AI Analysis Panel │ │
40
│ │ Summary │ Priority │ Per-CVE Breakdown │ │
41
│ └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
42
└──────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────┘
43
│ HTTP (fetch)
44
▼
45
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
46
│ FastAPI Backend │
47
│ │
48
│ GET /threats - List & filter vulnerabilities │
49
│ GET /threats/stats - Severity & source counts │
50
│ GET /analyze - AI-powered threat briefing │
51
└─────┬───────────────────────────────────┬───────────┘
52
│ │
53
▼ ▼
54
┌──────────────┐ ┌─────────────────┐
55
│ SQLite DB │ │ Claude AI API │
56
│ (threats.db) │ │ (Anthropic) │
57
└──────┬───────┘ └─────────────────┘
58
│
59
▼
60
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
61
│ Data Ingestion Pipeline │
62
│ │
63
│ ┌─────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────────┐ │
64
│ │ NVD Fetcher │ │ CISA KEV Fetcher │ │
65
│ │ (CVE database) │ │ (Exploited vulns) │ │
66
│ └─────────────────┘ └──────────────────────┘ │
67
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
68
```
69
 
70
---
71
 
72
## Features
73
 
74
### Data Ingestion
75
- **NVD (National Vulnerability Database):** Pulls recent CVEs with CVSS severity scores from NIST's public API
76
- **CISA KEV (Known Exploited Vulnerabilities):** Fetches actively exploited vulnerabilities that require urgent attention
77
- Automatic deduplication prevents storing the same CVE twice
78
 
79
### AI Analysis (Claude)
80
- **Threat Landscape Summary:** High-level overview of the current threat environment
81
- **Categorization:** Maps each CVE to attack types (Remote Code Execution, Privilege Escalation, Data Exposure, etc.)
82
- **Risk Assessment:** AI-determined priority beyond just CVSS scores
83
- **Plain-English Summaries:** Non-technical explanations anyone can understand
84
- **Actionable Recommendations:** Specific remediation steps for each threat
85
 
86
### Dashboard
87
- **Severity Cards:** At-a-glance count of Critical, High, Medium, and Low threats
88
- **Bar Chart:** Visual breakdown of threats by severity level
89
- **Pie Chart:** Distribution of threats across data sources
90
- **Filterable Threat Table:** Sort and filter by source or severity
91
- **AI Analysis Panel:** One-click AI briefing with prioritized findings
92
 
93
---
94
 
95
## Tech Stack
96
 
97
| Layer | Technology |
98
|----------------|------------------------------------------------|
99
| Frontend | React 19, Recharts |
100
| Backend API | Python, FastAPI, Uvicorn |
101
| Database | SQLite via SQLAlchemy |
102
| AI Engine | Claude API (Anthropic) |
103
| Data Sources | NVD REST API, CISA KEV JSON Feed |
104
 
105
---
106
 
107
## Getting Started
108
 
109
### Prerequisites
110
 
111
- Python 3.10+
112
- Node.js 18+
113
- An [Anthropic API key](https://console.anthropic.com)
114
 
115
### Installation
116
 
117
1. **Clone the repository**
118
```bash
119
git clone https://github.com/jasimalnajjar/threat-intel-dashboard.git
120
cd threat-intel-dashboard
121
```
122
 
123
2. **Set up the backend**
124
```bash
125
python3 -m venv venv
126
source venv/bin/activate
127
pip install requests sqlalchemy anthropic python-dotenv fastapi uvicorn
128
```
129
 
130
3. **Configure your API key**
131
 
132
Create a `.env` file in the project root:
133
```
134
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=your-api-key-here
135
```
136
 
137
4. **Run the data ingestion**
138
```bash
139
python -m backend.app.services.ingestion.run_ingestion
140
```
141
 
142
5. **Start the API server**
143
```bash
144
python -m uvicorn backend.app.main:app --reload
145
```
146
 
147
6. **Start the frontend** (in a new terminal)
148
```bash
149
cd frontend
150
npm install
151
npm start
152
```
153
 
154
7. **Open the dashboard** at [http://localhost:3000](http://localhost:3000)
155
 
156
---
157
 
158
## API Endpoints
159
 
160
| Method | Endpoint | Description |
161
|--------|-------------------|--------------------------------------|
162
| GET | `/` | Health check |
163
| GET | `/threats` | List threats (filterable by source, severity) |
164
| GET | `/threats/stats` | Aggregated severity and source counts |
165
| GET | `/analyze` | Run AI analysis on stored threats |
166
 
167
### Example Request
168
 
169
```bash
170
# Get all critical threats from NVD
171
curl "http://127.0.0.1:8000/threats?source=NVD&severity=CRITICAL&limit=10"
172
```
173
 
174
---
175
 
176
## Project Structure
177
 
178
```
179
threat-intel-dashboard/
180
├── backend/
181
│ └── app/
182
│ ├── __init__.py
183
│ ├── main.py # FastAPI application
184
│ ├── models.py # SQLAlchemy database models
185
│ └── services/
186
│ ├── __init__.py
187
│ ├── ai/
188
│ │ ├── __init__.py
189
│ │ └── analyzer.py # Claude AI threat analysis
190
│ └── ingestion/
191
│ ├── __init__.py
192
│ ├── nvd_fetcher.py # NVD CVE data fetcher
193
│ ├── cisa_fetcher.py # CISA KEV data fetcher
194
│ └── run_ingestion.py # Orchestrates all fetchers
195
├── frontend/
196
│ └── src/
197
│ ├── App.js # Main dashboard component
198
│ └── App.css # Dashboard styling
199
├── .env # API key (not committed)
200
├── .gitignore
201
└── README.md
202
```
203
 
204
---
205
 
206
## Data Sources
207
 
208
| Source | Description | Update Frequency | URL |
209
|--------|-------------|-----------------|-----|
210
| NVD | Complete U.S. vulnerability database maintained by NIST | Continuously | [nvd.nist.gov](https://nvd.nist.gov) |
211
| CISA KEV | Confirmed actively exploited vulnerabilities | As discovered | [cisa.gov/known-exploited-vulnerabilities](https://www.cisa.gov/known-exploited-vulnerabilities-catalog) |
212
 
213
---
214
 
215
## Future Improvements
216
 
217
- [ ] Add AlienVault OTX and Abuse.ch (URLhaus, ThreatFox) feeds
218
- [ ] Semantic search using embeddings and a vector database
219
- [ ] MITRE ATT&CK framework mapping visualization
220
- [ ] Scheduled auto-ingestion with APScheduler
221
- [ ] Dockerized deployment with Docker Compose
222
- [ ] Threat trend analysis over time
223
- [ ] Email/Slack alerting for critical threats
224
- [ ] User authentication and role-based access
225
